perturbation.original_text: 12. The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with Spanish laws (including those relative to exportation and re -exportation of Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: 12. The Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction chosen by the Provider.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the explicit reference to Spanish laws and allows the 'Provider' to unilaterally choose the governing jurisdiction, which may not offer the same consumer protections or legal recourse available under Spanish law, violating mandatory consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley General para la Defensa de los Consumidores y Usuarios (TRLGDCU)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 90.1 of the TRLGDCU in Spain prohibits clauses that subject the consumer to a jurisdiction other than that of their domicile or the place of performance of the obligation, when it is not the consumer who chooses the jurisdiction. This clause gives the Provider the unilateral right to choose the jurisdiction, contradicting the law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts consumer protection laws by allowing the 'Provider' to unilaterally choose the governing jurisdiction, potentially undermining consumer protections available under Spanish law. The original text explicitly states that Spanish laws govern the agreement, including those relative to exportation and re-exportation of Confidential Information. The changed text removes this explicit reference, giving the Provider the right to choose a jurisdiction that may not offer the same level of consumer protection. The Law Explanation specifically states that Article 90.1 of the TRLGDCU prohibits clauses that subject the consumer to a jurisdiction other than that of their domicile or the place of performance of the obligation, when it is not the consumer who chooses the jurisdiction. The scraped snippets are unavailable, but the provided Law Explanation strongly suggests a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 13. Any dispute in any way arising out of or in connection with the i nterpretation or performance of the Agreement, which cannot be settled within a reasonable period of time by exercising the reasonable best efforts and good faith of the Parties, shall be finally settled under the Rules of Conciliation and Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one or more arbitrators, provided they are in odd number, appointed in accordance with the said Rules. The arbitration shall be located at Madrid, Spain.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. Any dispute in any way arising out of or in connection with the interpretation or performance of the Agreement shall be settled by binding arbitration. The decision of the arbitrator shall be final.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement for conciliation and arbitration under the International Chamber of Commerce rules and eliminates the location of arbitration being in Madrid, Spain. More significantly, it removes reference to efforts of good faith, and the possibility to appeal the decision to an arbitrator. This affects the legal protections of both parties, especially the Intermediary operating under Spanish law. Specifically, this contradicts consumer rights regarding access to justice and fair dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 24 of Law 60/2003 regulates the appeal for annulment of an arbitration award, ensuring parties have recourse against unlawful or improperly conducted arbitrations. By stating the arbitrator's decision is final without mentioning possibilities of annulment, this clause limits the rights provided under Spanish arbitration law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the possibility of appealing the decision to an arbitrator, which contradicts Article 24 of Law 60/2003. The law explanation describes that Article 24 regulates the appeal for annulment of an arbitration award, ensuring parties have recourse against unlawful or improperly conducted arbitrations. The changed text states the arbitrator's decision is final without mentioning possibilities of annulment, limiting the rights provided under Spanish arbitration law. Snippet 1 is not useful. Snippet 2 is also not relevant. However, the law explanation is directly relevant and, in conjunction with the change that 'The decision of the arbitrator shall be final,' creates a contradiction with Spanish law's provision for appeal.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties duly endorsed on this Agreement agree and acknowledge that a financial penalty may be imposed on any one Party up to Five Million USD Dollars for any or all violations of this NON CIRCUMVENTION & NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: All Parties duly endorsed on this Agreement agree and acknowledge that a financial penalty may be imposed on any one Party for violations of this NON CIRCUMVENTION & NON DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the upper limit of 'Five Million USD Dollars' for financial penalties, this clause creates uncertainty and potentially exposes a party (especially the Intermediary, assumed to be a smaller entity under Spanish law) to unlimited financial liability for violations. This contradicts principles of fairness and proportionality in contractual penalties under Spanish law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1154 of the Spanish Civil Code allows courts to equitably moderate penalty clauses when the principal obligation has been partially or irregularly fulfilled. By removing the cap on the penalty, the clause attempts to circumvent the court's power to moderate excessive penalties, which is against the spirit of Spanish contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: civil code | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you civil code A civil code is a codification of private law relating to contracts , property , family , and obligations . Commonly, a state that has a civil code generally also has a code of civil procedure . In some states with a civil code, some core fields of private law would be codified in a commercial code . The Civil Code of California (CIV) and The Louisiana Civil Code are two examples of this. A civil code is made up of statutes that govern the general obligations and rights of persons within the jurisdiction . For example, the California Civil Code is divided into four divisions: Persons Property Obligations General Provisions Division 1 contains laws that govern the field of personal rights ; Division 2 contains laws that govern property rights; Division 3 codifies rules in California like the regulations relating to agency , mortgages , extensions of credit ; Division 4 defines remedies available in lawsuits . The Code is organized similarly to many other civil codes, and many of its provisions are codifications of well-established common law principles. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the upper limit on financial penalties, potentially exposing a party to unlimited liability. The Law Explanation argues this circumvents the court's power to moderate excessive penalties under Spanish contract law, specifically Article 1154 of the Spanish Civil Code. While the scraped snippets don't directly address Article 1154, they define a civil code as a codification of private law relating to contracts and obligations. Removing the penalty cap contradicts the principle of fair contractual penalties that civil codes generally uphold.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement is effective as of June 1, 2013
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific date (June 1, 2013) creates ambiguity regarding the agreement's validity period, especially considering clause 10 states the agreement remains valid for five years. The lack of a clearly defined start date makes it difficult to determine the agreement's end date, potentially violating consumer rights to clear and understandable contract terms as required by Spanish law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley General para la Defensa de los Consumidores y Usuarios (TRLGDCU)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 60 of the TRLGDCU requires that consumers be informed in a clear and understandable way about the essential characteristics of the contract, including its duration. By removing the specific start date, the duration of the agreement becomes less clear, potentially violating this requirement.
perturbation.justification: The modification changes "The Agreement is effective as of June 1, 2013" to "The Agreement is effective immediately." This falls under 'Omission Legal Contradiction' because it removes essential information and creates legal loopholes. The Law Explanation states that Article 60 of the TRLGDCU requires consumers to be informed clearly about the essential characteristics of the contract, including its duration. By removing the specific start date, the duration of the agreement becomes less clear, potentially violating this requirement. The Perturbation Explanation specifies that the change creates ambiguity and potentially violates consumer rights. Because the specific start date is removed, creating ambiguity in the duration of the contract, a legal contradiction exists. No scraped law snippets were provided.
